topic: Traits - Index Trait for Custom Indexing
content: When you wrap a collection like Vec or String in a newtype, your goal is to make it look and behave exactly like the underlying container while hiding its details. The Rust way to achieve this is by implementing the Index trait so that array-like syntax works with your custom type. You might be tempted to simply return the inner value from your index operation, but that approach fails because the compiler expects an output type that matches what you are indexing into, not just any compatible value. For instance if you have a NewVec struct wrapping Vec<i32> and try impl Index<usize> for NewVec where Self: From<Vec<i32>> { fn index(&self, _index: usize) -> Result<_, Error> { Ok(self.inner()[_index]) } }, this will likely cause type errors because the trait requires returning an element of the same type as the collection being indexed, not a generic result or wrapped value. The correct implementation must return Self::output_type which for most standard collections is the element type itself so NewVec.index(0) returns i32 rather than Result<i32, Error>. This distinction between returning the actual element versus wrapping it in another type is a common pitfall that trips up beginners who assume any valid Rust value can be returned from an index method. Beyond just reading elements you often need to support modification through IndexMut which lets you use assignment syntax like my_custom_vec[0] = 42 instead of calling a separate set method. Implementing IndexMut requires returning the mutable reference to the inner collection so that your custom type delegates mutations directly without creating intermediate copies or requiring extra methods on your wrapper struct. A subtle misconception many developers encounter is thinking they can implement both traits independently and then rely on the compiler to figure out how they interact, but in reality you must ensure your Index implementation returns exactly the same output type that your IndexMut implementation mutates so that expressions like vec[0] = x remain consistent with the language rules. If you forget to match these types correctly your code will fail to compile even if your logic seems sound because Rust enforces strict type consistency across trait implementations. Another frequent issue involves forgetting to handle bounds properly when dealing with different container types; for example trying to implement Index<usize> for a generic NewCollection<T> without requiring T: Clone or other specific traits can lead to unexpected behavior when combined with operations like push_back or extend_on_index. Finally, consider the scenario where you want slice-like access but your collection is not actually a Vec or String; in such cases you must explicitly define what output_type should be for your Index trait and ensure your IndexMut returns a mutable reference to that exact type so that the compiler understands the relationship between read and write operations. By carefully aligning these two traits with the correct output types and understanding how they interact under the hood, your custom collection will seamlessly integrate into expressions involving indexing while maintaining safety and predictability.
